query: vibe coding complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Am I the issue or is vibe coding just the most frustrating and un-enjoyable thing ever? : r/vibecoding
results.content:
And that's one of my main issues with vibe coding or AI in general, it is so unpredictable and unreliable. You don't know whether it will help or it will output the most hallucinated thing ever that has nothing to do with my issue, and I don't like that. I like having control over the output and not having just feels so unnatural and weird. I don't get it, is my ChatGPT broken? Or my Claude? How are people making anything usable with AI when I can't even get it to center an HTML div. I hear a lot of the time "You just don't know how to prompt!", my brother in Christ I don't think it's rocket science. I've tried breaking down the prompt into tiny pieces, I've tried asking it what information it needs, I've given context, screenshots, etc.... And it still sucks, but for little scripts and [...] It’s frustrating enough as someone who’s technically literate enough to know the stupid reasons why the AI is wrong and what it should’ve done.

Can’t imagine vibecoders frustration when they arnt even able to fix the problems and have to spend money on a whole other AI to try and correct the other AI. And then another AI for that one ad infinitum

As someone who's doing this- it is very frustrating but there are also some profound moments as well- I think it's only inspired me to actually learn to code. I see it as something possible now for me, whereas before I didn't consider myself capable, or that "i should've learned it x years ago' mentality creeps in. That's gone now- I'm open to it, because of llm's.

I may be off but I think the more controlling we try to be as the human half, the worse our code buddy half performs. I get significantly better output when I am open to creative freedom.

[](

Tim-Sylvester

•10mo ago

In my experience the biggest source of frustration with vibe coding is when I get lazy and don't build the prompt to provide sufficient context and instruction for the agent to do what I actually want it to. I actually just published an article about this this week.

The frustration is borne of the agent's inability to correctly infer what I'm leaving implicit. If I take away the implicitness and make it explicit, I have way less frustration for the agent.

results.title: The problem with "vibe coding" | Hacker News
results.content: frustrating, yet we're getting there through convolutated, inefficient and borderline barbaric means.  People are still lamenting after HyperCard. Automation on windows or macos didn't go anywhere. Shortcuts were a better step into that direction but I feel it got stuck in the middle as Apple wasn't going to push it further. Android had more freedom yet you won't see a "normal" user do automation either.  If we're going to point the middle finger at vibe-coding, I wish we had something to point to as a better tool for the general population to quickly make stuff they want/need.  (Doing it as a professional dev is to me another debate, still with nuance in it IMHO. I'd also love better prototyping tools to be honest.) | | | |  |  | skydhash on April 15, 2025  | parent | next [...] make the same mistakes again in the same circumstances. My main takeaway from vibe-coding is that nobody cared enough to fill that niche and expectation. And it was really frustrating, yet we're getting there through convolutated, [...] The argument is that vibe coding is great for little personal programs but bad for full-blown products. I don't think you can ship a fully baked product made exclusively with AI coding yet. But it's \really\ useful for investigative product development - e.g. when you have a few different ideas for how something might work but aren't quite sure what's best. I used to regret that I never mastered Figma or other mockup tools - now I never will have to. | | | |  |  | \_\_MatrixMan\_\_ on April 15, 2025  | prev | next (javascript:void(0))   This is about the problem with vibe coding your way to a product. But if the vibes are strong enough, maybe we don't need products at all? They do tend to complicate things, with all of their moats and such. I never wanted a product that did the thing, I

results.title: The illusion of a complete app: the problem with vibe coding | Roy Derks posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: 1,847 lines of code. A user reports a bug. You have no idea how any of it actually works. This is where vibe coding dies. The app shipped fine. The demo looked great. Your non-technical friend thinks you're a wizard. Then reality hits. A bug needs fixing. A feature needs adding. Something breaks in production. You ask the AI to fix it. It introduces two new problems. You ask again. It breaks something else. The LLM has no memory of why it made those architectural decisions in the first place. The people who actually make this work? They're not really vibe coding. They're running multiple AI instances. One writes code. Another reviews it. A third checks for issues. They're creating documentation. Writing tests. Understanding every line before it ships. That's not vibe coding. That's [...] 1,847 lines of code. A user reports a bug. You have no idea how any of it actually works. This is where vibe coding dies. The app shipped fine. The demo looked great. Your non-technical friend thinks you're a wizard. Then reality hits. A bug needs fixing. A feature needs adding. Something breaks in production. You ask the AI to fix it. It introduces two new problems. You ask again. It breaks something else. The LLM has no memory of why it made those architectural decisions in the first place. The people who actually make this work? They're not really vibe coding. They're running multiple AI instances. One writes code. Another reviews it. A third checks for issues. They're creating documentation. Writing tests. Understanding every line before it ships. That's not vibe coding. That's [...] The biggest problem with vibe coding is perception. Non-technical people often see a working application and assume it’s done. But every developer knows: what looks like a “complete app” could have a bowl spaghetti underneath. It might: - use a database schema that won’t scale past 100 records - lack authentication or input validation - call APIs in ways that will break under real traffic - have no logging, tests, or CI/CD pipeline - or rely on 800 lines of Docker config to deploy a handful of fragile services. To someone who has never written a line of code, that still looks like a finished product. To an engineer, it’s a tech-debt time bomb waiting for the first real users that break it on a Saturday. AI is great at helping developers building better software, but not (yet) for letting

results.title: The problem with vibe coding is nobody wants to talk about maintenance : r/vibecoding
results.content:
> The dirty secret nobody mentions: vibe coding is fantastic for prototypes and throwaway projects. It's terrible for anything you actually need to maintain.

I agree that vibe coding becomes less viable the larger the scope becomes, but there's plenty of middle ground between "throwaway prototype" and a big complex app.

No-Spirit1451

•8mo ago

The problem isn't vibe coding, it's people treating code generation as the final step instead of the first. If you're not reviewing output, handling edge cases, and understanding architecture as you build, you'd have the same maintenance nightmare writing it manually. AI doesn't excuse you from actually engineering the product. What's your actual point?

[](

mxldevs

•8mo ago

The point of vibe coding is to let AI handle everything.

Technical debt doesn't exist when the AI can rebuild the entire app in minutes with the new features and passes all the tests that gives it the greenlight.

No different from you hiring a coder and sending their code back everytime it fails to meet your requirements.

results.title: Vibe Coding Is The WORST IDEA Of 2025 - YouTube
results.content:
us to verify that it does it. Programming moves then from specifying
implementation detail to more clearly and more precisely specifying the intent
of the system that we want to build. If you'd like to learn more about how to do
that, check out my training course, Acceptance Testing BDD from Stories to
Executable Specifications, which includes an example of programming out
with AI like this. So, Vibe coding doesn't address any of these problems.
It's simply not good enough. Software engineering isn't dying, it's changing.
I'd argue that these three problems are fundamental to the nature of code, AI
generated or not. And any next step in programming must address all of them or
